title: Fix eval_eq/eval_cmp to check Scalar dimensions
analysis: eval_eq() at lib.rs:321-334 handles Bool, Int, String explicitly but Scalar falls through to the wildcard `_ =>` arm which calls as_f64() on both sides—stripping the dimension. eval_cmp() at lib.rs:343-347 always calls as_f64() on both sides, also ignoring dimension. This means 5mm == 5kg evaluates to true and 3mm < 5kg evaluates to true. The fix is to add explicit (Value::Scalar, Value::Scalar) match arms in both functions that compare dimensions first. For eval_eq: return Bool(false) on dimension mismatch, then compare si_value. For eval_cmp: return Undef on dimension mismatch (comparing incompatible units is undefined), then compare si_value with the closure. The existing eval_ne() delegates to eval_eq(), so it automatically benefits. Cross-type Scalar-vs-Int/Real comparisons must also be addressed: the current as_f64() fallback treats Int/Real as dimensionless, which is arguably correct for DIMENSIONLESS Scalars but wrong for dimensioned ones. Dimensioned Scalar vs non-Scalar should return Bool(false) for eq and Undef for cmp. DIMENSIONLESS Scalars should continue falling through to as_f64(). The existing test `gt_comparison` (line 482) compares two same-dimension LENGTH scalars via eval_cmp—this already works and must continue working. The existing patterns for dimensional checks (eval_add/eval_sub check dimension equality returning Undef on mismatch at lines 137-179) provide the template.

steps.description: Write failing test `eq_scalar_different_dimensions_is_false`: construct two Scalar literals with same si_value (0.005) but different dimensions (LENGTH vs MASS), build CompiledExpr::binop(BinOp::Eq, ...), assert eval_expr returns Bool(false). This currently returns Bool(true) because as_f64() strips dimension.

Write test `eq_scalar_same_dimension_same_value_is_true`: construct two LENGTH Scalars with identical si_value (0.08), build Eq binop, assert eval_expr returns Bool(true). This should already pass via as_f64() fallback but documents correct behavior.

Write test `eq_scalar_same_dimension_different_value_is_false`: construct two LENGTH Scalars with different si_values (0.08 vs 0.10), build Eq binop, assert eval_expr returns Bool(false). Should already pass.

Write failing test `cmp_scalar_different_dimensions_is_undef`: construct two Scalars with same si_value but different dimensions (LENGTH vs MASS), build CompiledExpr::binop(BinOp::Lt, ...), assert eval_expr returns Undef. Currently returns Bool(false) because as_f64() compares 0.005 < 0.005.

Write test `cmp_scalar_same_dimension_works`: construct two LENGTH Scalars (3mm=0.003 vs 5mm=0.005), build Lt binop, assert eval_expr returns Bool(true). Should already pass (and existing gt_comparison test covers this too).

Write failing test `eq_dimensioned_scalar_vs_int_is_false`: construct Scalar{si_value: 5.0, dimension: LENGTH} and Int(5), build Eq binop, assert eval_expr returns Bool(false). Currently returns Bool(true) because both as_f64() return Some(5.0).

Write test `ne_scalar_different_dimensions_is_true`: construct two Scalars with different dimensions, build Ne binop, assert eval_expr returns Bool(true). Verifies eval_ne correctly inverts the fixed eval_eq. Currently fails (returns Bool(false)).

Add explicit Scalar match arm in eval_eq before the wildcard: match (Value::Scalar { si_value: a, dimension: da }, Value::Scalar { si_value: b, dimension: db }) => if da != db { Bool(false) } else { Bool(a == b) }. Also add arms for dimensioned Scalar vs non-Scalar: (Value::Scalar { dimension, .. }, _) | (_, Value::Scalar { dimension, .. }) where !dimension.is_dimensionless() => Bool(false). DIMENSIONLESS Scalars continue to fall through to the as_f64 wildcard. This fixes steps 1, 6, and 7.

Add explicit Scalar match arm in eval_cmp before the as_f64 fallback: match (Value::Scalar { si_value: a, dimension: da }, Value::Scalar { si_value: b, dimension: db }) => if da != db { Undef } else { Bool(cmp(*a, *b)) }. Also add arms for dimensioned Scalar vs non-Scalar: (Value::Scalar { dimension, .. }, _) | (_, Value::Scalar { dimension, .. }) where !dimension.is_dimensionless() => Undef. DIMENSIONLESS Scalars continue to fall through to as_f64. This fixes step 4.

Run `cargo test -p reify-expr` to verify all new and existing tests pass (including existing gt_comparison which tests same-dimension cmp). Then run `cargo test --workspace` to ensure no regressions across the project.

design_decisions.decision: Scalar-vs-Scalar with mismatched dimensions: eval_eq returns Bool(false), eval_cmp returns Undef
design_decisions.rationale: Two values with different physical dimensions are definitively not equal (false is correct), but ordering them is meaningless (Undef is correct—you can't say 5mm < 5kg). This matches the existing pattern where add/sub of mismatched dimensions returns Undef, and is consistent with the strict Undef propagation contract.

design_decisions.decision: Dimensioned Scalar vs bare Int/Real: eval_eq returns Bool(false), eval_cmp returns Undef
design_decisions.rationale: A dimensioned quantity like 5mm should not equal the bare number 5. Int and Real are implicitly dimensionless—comparing them to a dimensioned Scalar is a type mismatch. Only DIMENSIONLESS Scalars should compare against Int/Real via the existing as_f64 fallback.

design_decisions.decision: DIMENSIONLESS Scalar vs Int/Real continues to use as_f64 comparison
design_decisions.rationale: A DIMENSIONLESS Scalar is conceptually just a real number, so comparing it to Int/Real via as_f64 is correct and preserves backward compatibility. The guard uses dimension.is_dimensionless() which already exists on DimensionVector.

design_decisions.decision: Use match guards with is_dimensionless() rather than separate DIMENSIONLESS match arms
design_decisions.rationale: Match guards like `(Value::Scalar { dimension, .. }, _) if !dimension.is_dimensionless()` are more readable and maintainable than matching on the internal array representation of DimensionVector. The is_dimensionless() method already exists and is well-tested.

reuse.how: Reuse existing mm_val(v: f64) -> Value helper to create LENGTH scalars in tests
Reuse existing lit(v: Value, ty: Type) -> CompiledExpr helper to wrap values as literal expressions
Use existing constants for constructing test Scalar values and for dimension comparisons
Use in match guards to distinguish dimensioned vs dimensionless Scalars for cross-type comparison fallthrough

Follow same pattern: create lit() expressions, build CompiledExpr::binop, call eval_expr, match result. The mass Scalar construction pattern from add_different_dimensions_is_undef is directly reusable.
eval_ne already delegates to eval_eq and inverts the result. Fixing eval_eq automatically fixes eval_ne—no changes needed there.
